text:
O  In recent years, interest in using aerial robots, commonly
R known as unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs), across diverse
.
s applications has experienced fast growth. The multifaceted
c capabilities of UAVs—vertical takeoff and landing, hovering
[
  with directional  changes,  and lateral  and altitude adjust-
 
1 ments—have opened an extensive array of  applications,
v  including surveillance, photography, rescue missions, cargo
5 delivery, and so on [1],  [2]. Notably,  the realm of cargo
3
transportation has gained substantial popularity, evidenced by
6
major online retailers like Amazon, Google, DHL, and Wal-
7
mart announcing the incorporation of UAVs into their parcel
0
.
delivery operations [1]. Utilizing a cable to connect a UAV
4
and its load is efficient and cost-effective, but it introduces
0
4 greater control challenges for UAVs due to the potential
2 swinging of the cargo, which can impact UAV dynamics [3].
:
Consequently, a crucial aspect of UAV transportation control
v
i  is to limit the swing and oscillations of the load suspended
X beneath the UAV. In this paper, we use the term ”cargo UAV”
r
to denote a UAV with a cable-suspended load beneath it.
a
Controlling UAVs poses challenges due to the nonlinearity
of their dynamics and the under-actuation. A UAV’s control
typically involves managing attitude and altitude  through
four thrust inputs. Researchers have made several efforts in
developing control systems, such as proportional-derivative
controllers, linear quadratic regulators (LQR), and nonlinear
controllers [4]. Among these, a geometric controller pro-
posed in [5] stands out. It expressed UAV dynamics globally
1
Yuxia  Yuan  is  with  Autonomous  Aerial  Systems,  School
of  Engineering  and  Design,  Technical  University  of  Mu-
nich,  Lise-Meitner-Straße  9,  85521  Ottobrunn,  Germany
yuxia.yuan@tum.de,yuxia.yuan2@outlook.com
2
Markus Ryll is with Munich Institute of Robotics and Machine Intel-
ligence (MIRMI) and Autonomous Aerial Systems, School of Engineering
and Design, Technical University of Munich, Lise-Meitner-Straße 9, 85521
Ottobrunn, Germany markus.ryll@tum.de

Vs with Cable-suspended Load
2
Markus Ryll
Fig. 1: Dual quaternion transformations among three frames. RI is
the inertial frame, qˆv denotes the transformation from RI  to Rv
l
frame, and qˆl is the transformation from RI to Rl. qˆv indicates the
transformation from Rl to Rv.
on the configuration manifold of the special Euclidean group
SE(3). Compared to other geometric control approaches, it
performed well  in controlling an underactuated UAV. The
reseachers then further extended their work on a cargo UAV
system, defined on the configuration space SE(3) ×S2 [6].
This geometric controller was then widely applied to cargo
UAV control [3], [7], [8]. For example, the authors proposed
a hybrid model in [7] to lift a cable-suspended load based
on this geometric controller. They decomposed the lifting
maneuver into three modes in this study: Setup, Pull, and
Raise to formulate the dynamics of the cargo UAV system
separately.
Further explorations into cargo UAV systems include LQR
control algorithm [9], decoupling of cargo UAV dynamics in
longitudinal and lateral planes [10], and a fixed-time control
approach to enhance system robustness considering external
disturbances [11]. Additionally, researchers also trained neu-
ral networks for cargo UAV control, as demonstrated in [12],
where a convolutional neural network was used to estimate
the position and orientation of the load.
This paper introduces a novel control strategy for cable-
suspended UAVs using dual  quaternions  to enhance  the
efficiency and compactness of sling load lifting and trajectory
tracking. Dual quaternion transformations between different
frames are shown in Fig. 1, which provides a mathematical
framework that allows us to represent both the dynamics
and kinematics of the UAV and its sling load in a unified
representation. Dual quaternions can be seen as an extension
of unit quaternions to represent the position and orientation
of a rigid body [13]. They provide several advantages com-
pared to homogeneous transformation matrices [14], [5] for
the representation of pose transformation,  such as higher
computational  efficiency, mathematical  compactness,  lack
of singularities, and optimal rigid transformation blending.
These features of dual quaternions have been used for the

A. Assumptions and Notations
The cargo UAV studied in our work consists of a UAV
and a sling load. It can be considered as two subsystems and
each of them is a rigid body, which involves three orientation
frames, as shown in Fig. 1. The load is physically attached
to the UAV by a cable. Modeling all  internal and external
forces can quickly become very complex. For this study, we
made the following simplifications and assumptions:

1) We ignore elastic deformations of the rope, the UAV,
and the load.
2) The load is considered as a point mass.
3) The cable is mass-free, and only forces and no torques
are transmitted via the cable.
4) The offset between the attached point of the load with
the CoM of the UAV is considered as zero.
5) The air drag and other disturbances are ignored.
Before going further,  the  following notation rules are
adopted:
v,l ,c  Subscripts indicate UAVs, loads, and cables.
d
Superscript d represents the desired states.
b
Superscripts b indicate the body frame.
q, qˆ ∈ H  Quaternions and dual quaternions.
3
v ∈ R  Vectors (vector quaternions).
vˆ ∈ H  Dual vector quaternions.
I , 0 ∈ H Unit quaternion [1, 0, 0, 0] and zero quaternion.
0 ∈ H  Zero vector quaternion.
B. Dual Quaternion
As described in [13], the components of a dual quaternion
are both quaternions belonging to H, which is represented as
qˆ = qr + qdϵ, qr, qd ∈ H. The involved operations for dual
quaternions are listed below:
• Sum: qˆ1 + qˆ2 = q1r + q2r + [q1d + q2d]ϵ
• Product: qˆ1 ⊗ qˆ2 = q1r ⊗ q2r + [q1r ⊗ q2d + q1d ⊗ q2r]ϵ
∗  ∗  ∗
• Conjugate: qˆ = qr + qdϵ
2
• Norm for unit dual quaternions: ||qˆ|| = 1 + 0ϵ
−1  ∗
•  Inverse for unit dual quaternions: qˆ  = qˆ .
1
• Natural logarithm mapping: ln qˆ =  (θ + Tϵ); θ is the
2
rotation angle vector.
∗
• Adjoint transformation: Adqˆrˆ = qˆ ⊗ rˆ ⊗ qˆ
• Dual vector quaternion: vˆ = vr +vdϵ. vˆ can be treated
as vˆ = [0, vr] + [0, vd]ϵ.
C. Dual Quaternion Dynamic Model
Using the Newton-Euler approach, the rotational dynamics
of a rigid body can be expressed as
b  b  b
Jω˙  + ω × (Jω ) = τ  (1)

3

torques. Similarly,  the translational dynamics relative to its
body frame is given by
¨ b

control input forces and external forces (e.g., gravity) in the
body frame.
The kinematics of a rigid body in terms of its orientation,
position, and twist is given in [16] by

rotational velocity ω and translational velocity T . Then the

The cargo UAV system contains a sling load attached
to  the CoM of  the UAV, which can be separated  into
two subsystems,  the sling-load and the UAV,  to study its
dynamics. In the following, we develop cable state-dependent
scenarios to derive the complete model.
A. Dual Vector Quaternion of the Dynamics of the Sling-load
In our research,  the sling load is attached to the UAV
by a cable. We treat the load and the cable as a sling-load
subsystem. Though we assume the cable is rigid, there is no
torque transmitted by the cable to the load. The dual vector
quaternion qˆ is adopted to describe the configuration of the
sling load as
qˆl = qc +Tlϵ  (10)
ξˆ = qˆ˙ = q˙  +T˙  ϵ,  (11)

where qc is the direction vector from the UAV to the load
and Tl  is the position of the load in the initial frame.
B. Dynamic Model with Slack Cable
When the cable is slack, the UAV and the sling load can
be considered as two separate subsystems. The kinematics
and dynamics of the UAV can refer to the rigid body model
formulation in Sec. II-C. For the sling load subsystem it is

static on the ground or in free fall. The motion equations of

C. Dynamic Model with Taut Cable
When the cable is taut, the configuration of the system is
defined by the position and attitude of the load with respect
to the inertial  frame, and the UAV attitude. The position
and velocity relationships between the UAV and the load are
expressed as
Tv = Tl + lqc  (16)

The motion equations of the sling load are obtained from

metadata.section_title: Control Design for Cargo UAV System

text:
A. Lifting Modes and Desired Motion States
The lifting maneuver is sketched in Fig. 2, which is broken
into three modes: Setup, Pull, and Raise.  In this way,  the
dynamics of the cargo UAV system in specific regimes during
the maneuver can be characterized separately according to
the state of the cable. During the Setup and Pull modes, the
makes preparations to lift the load, and in the Raise mode,
the load is finally lifted off the ground.
Setup: As shown in Fig. 2, the UAV has to fly to the point
above the load from its initial position. During the process,
the cable is slack, so the system can be considered as two
separate subsystems. The dynamics of the UAV and the load
are given in Sec. III-B, while the load remains static.
The UAV is expected to reach the point right above the
load with the distance between them as the cable length.
d.s
Then, the desired configuration of the UAV is given by qv
d.s
and Tv  in Table I.
The cargo UAV system turns  to Pull mode when the
distance between the UAV and the load is equal to the cable
length l, which means the cable is fully extended.  It can
be expressed as l = ||Tv − Tl||. To avoid swinging when
lifting the load, we also consider that the UAV should be in
ˆb
a stable condition, which is measured by the errors (qve, ξve)
converging to (I, 0ˆ).
Pull: Even though the cable is not slack anymore after
Setup mode, the force on the cable is initially not sufficient
to lift the load. Therefore, the load is still static in dynamic
model (21). We assume the cargo UAV turns into Raise mode
until ||Tv−Tl|| = l and mvg ≤ ||FuI ||, where l is the cable
length.
Raise: The UAV is ready to raise the sling load when
l = ||Tv − Tl||. During this mode,  the load is not static
anymore. The motion equations for the cargo UAV system
are as well coupled as described in Sec. III-C. From pulling
until the end of raising, the load is expected to reach a desired
height, which is given by H in Table I. The corresponding
d.r  ˙ d.r
desired states are given by Tl  and Tl  .
B. Control Law for Slack Cable
When the cable is slack during Setup,  the load remains
static on the ground. We just consider the control of the UAV.
A control law coming from [15], [16] is adopted to cancel
ˆb  ˆ
the non-linearities of the UAV subsystem ξv = Fv +uˆv, and

C. Control Law for Taut Cable
We aim to track a desired trajectory with the sling load
while keeping the desired yaw angle of the UAV. The position
of  the sling load is governed by the control of  the UAV
and the direction vector of the cable. We define the desired
d  d  d
configuration and twist of the sling load as qˆl = qc +Tl ϵ
ˆd  d  ˙ d  d  ˙ d
and ξl = q˙ c+Tl ϵ, where Tl and Tl are the desired position
d
and velocity of the load. qc is the desired load attitude. The
desired yaw angle of the UAV is set as zero. From the dual
vector quaternion error dynamics of the sling load, we have
the tracking errors as
qˆle = qce +Tleϵ  (30)
ξˆ = q˙  +T˙  ϵ.  (31)
le  ce  le
where T  and T˙  are the load position and velocity errors.

expressed as

d  d

From the dynamic model (21), the control force FuI has
coupling terms between the UAV’s attitude and the load’s
position and attitude. Meanwhile, we can see that the UAV’s
attitude  is determined by its desired yaw angle and the
load’s position and attitude. Therefore, a controller for the
load’s attitude and position is designed as Eq. (34), and a
controller for the UAV’s attitude is designed considering the
dual quaternion error dynamics described in II-D as Eq. (35).

V. SIMULATION
A. Simulation Settings
The performance of the proposed control strategy is tested
in a set of numerical simulations with a time step of 0.01 s.
The parameters of the cargo UAV system, the control gains,
initial values, and parameters for the desired trajectories are
depicted in Table I.
1
Parameters in this paper, such as J, m, T (l), and ω, are all using
2
International System of Units (kg ∗ m , kg, m, and rad/s). The units are
omitted therein.

Fig. 3: States of the UAV during the lifting and trajectory tracking
process. In these figures, the vertical black dashed lines indicate the
time at which the UAV switches the mode to the next one, colored
lines show the real values and shade areas are the errors with the
desired values. The first figure depicts the position of the UAV and
the second one presents the translational velocity. The two figures
in the bottom row show the attitude and angular velocity of the
UAV.

Fig. 4: States of the load during the lifting and tracking process.
The vertical black dashed lines indicate the time at which the UAV
switches the mode to the next one. The two figures in the first
row show the position and translational velocity of the load. The
figures in the bottom row depict the unit direction vector qc and
its derivative.
B. Simulation 1: Lifting and Trajectory Tracking
In this simulation, we conduct  the tracking of a trajec-
tory including the take-off maneuver and the tracking of a
trajectory with the load. The sling load lifting maneuver is
simulated following the three modes [7] illustrated in Fig. 2
and explained in Sec.  IV-A. After  the sling load reaches
a desired height,  the reference trajectory is generated and
the sling load tracks the trajectory. The desired trajectory is
˙ d.t
determined by Tl  in Table I.
The simulation results are depicted in Fig. 3, Fig. 4, and
Fig. 5. The UAV takes about 3.9 s to finish the setup process.
Then,  in a very short  time,  the UAV completes  the Pull
mode (about 0.2 s) being in a stable state avoiding cable
oscillation [18]. The total  time of accomplishing the load
lifting maneuver  takes ≈6.9 s.  The results show that  the
UAV maintains stable status above the load throughout the
Setup and Pull phases. During these phases, the unit direction
vector qc adapts to the UAV’s position, as depicted in Fig. 4.
Upon entering the Raise mode,  the load ascends from the
ground and approaches the desired height. Notably, the errors
qce and q˙ ce in this mode remain close to zero,  indicating

supported by the velocity plots Fig. 6. The dual quater-
nion control yields smooth velocity control. The reason for
the fluctuation happening around 7 s might be the sudden
changes of velocities on the x-axis and y-axis, and the effects
of control gains. These results demonstrate that the sling is
able to track a desired trajectory with small tracking errors
and minimum swing using our developed controller.
VI. CONCLUSION
By utilizing the mathematical efficiency and compactness
of dual quaternions,  this study introduces a novel control
strategy, where the kinematics and dynamics of  the UAV
and its sling load are represented by dual quaternions. This
representation significantly improves the control performance
of the cargo UAV system. Two simulations were designed to
assess the proposed control strategy. The lifting and tracking
maneuver simulation results show that the sling load is able
to track a desired trajectory with small tracking errors and
swings. The second simulation indicates that  the proposed
control strategy is robust to system uncertainties and distur-
bances. The comparison results with the geometric controller
show that our controller is more stable and performs better
for tracking tasks. In future work, we will consider a potential
offset between the attachment point of the load and the CoM
of the UAV. Then, we will  test  the proposed controller on
real UAVs.
